questions.question: Select all that are true about why hash(key) mod N remaps almost every key when you add one server.
choices: Changing N changes the modulus for almost every key at once, so their destinations all shift.
Only the keys that land on the newly added server are rehashed.
The keys are stored in sorted order and must be re-sorted after a resize.
None of the above
questions.explanation: mod N ties every key's destination to the exact value of N, so a new divisor rewrites the remainder for keys all over the table. It is not limited to the new server and has nothing to do with sorting.

questions.question: Select all that are true about key ownership on a consistent-hashing ring.
choices: A key is owned by the first server clockwise from its position.
Adding one of N servers relocates only about K/N of the keys.
Adding a server recomputes the owner of every key on the ring.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Ownership is local to an arc, so a key belongs to the next server clockwise and a new server only steals its one arc, about K/N keys. Every other key's clockwise walk is unchanged.

questions.question: Select all that are true about virtual nodes on the ring.
choices: Each server hashes to many replica points scattered around the ring.
The many small arcs owned by a server average out to a fair share of keys.
When a server leaves, its arcs spill to several different neighbors rather than one successor.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Virtual nodes give each server many replicas and therefore many small arcs, which balances the key count and spreads a departing server's load across several neighbors.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about what the consistent-hashing ring guarantees.
choices: It balances the TRAFFIC per key, so a single hot key cannot overload its owner.
It balances the NUMBER of keys per server.
With one point per server the arcs are always perfectly even.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The ring balances key COUNT, not traffic, so a hot key still hammers its owner, and one point per server gives uneven arcs. Those two claims are false. The key-count claim is true.

questions.question: Select all that are true about how the token bucket refills.
choices: On each request it adds elapsed-time times the refill rate, capped at capacity.
It stores just the current token count and the last-request timestamp per client.
A background job tops up every bucket once per second.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Lazy refill computes the drip on demand from two stored numbers when a request arrives, capped at capacity. No background timer runs.

questions.question: Select all that are true about a token bucket that never caps tokens at capacity.
choices: It still enforces the same maximum burst as a capped bucket.
It is the recommended default configuration.
The cap has no effect on the size of a burst.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Without the cap an idle client accumulates thousands of tokens and can unleash a burst that swamps the service, so the cap IS the maximum allowed burst. None of the listed statements is true.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the rate-limiting family taught in this lesson.
choices: The token bucket allows a bounded burst up to capacity while enforcing a long-run average.
A sliding-window counter enforces a strict rolling count with no saved-up burst.
A leaky bucket drains queued requests at a fixed rate, smoothing output but adding latency.
Ten independent local limiters automatically enforce a correct global limit.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The three algorithms trade burst tolerance against smoothness as described. Ten independent local limiters do not enforce a global limit under lopsided traffic, so that claim is false.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the distributed-systems guarantees in this lesson.
choices: R + W > N guarantees a read set and a write set share at least one node, which holds the latest write.
During a partition only the side with a strict majority may accept writes, and two majorities always overlap.
Both sides of a partition may keep accepting writes safely.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Two subsets of N whose sizes sum to more than N must overlap, so a read sees the latest write, and only the overlapping majority side may progress. Letting both sides write causes split brain.
